content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660       322-5660         322-5901          322-6441           322-6444

October 17, 1980


Robert E. Roney
Portola City Attorney
P.O. Box 225
Portola, CA 96122

Dear Mr. Roney:

     This is in response to your letter of September 24, 
1980, on behalf of Portola Mayor Thomas Hervey and Council-
member Glen Howard. The following advice is provided pursuant 
to Government Code Section 83114(b). The facts as I understand 
them from your letter and from our telephone conversation of 
October 16, 1980, are as follows.

     Mr. Hervey and Mr. Howard own approximately three acres 
and one acre, respectively, of unimproved land adjacent to a 
320 acre uninhabited parcel currently owned by the Forest 
Service. The current value of their land is approximately 
$10,000 per acre. A local real estate broker has lands 
suitable for a land exchange to enable him to acquire the 
320 acre parcel. He has approached the City concerning an 
annexation of the 320 acre site in connection with a proposed 
subdivision, which at full buildout would contain approximately 
1,200 living units. You have asked whether Mr. Hervey and 
Mr. Howard can participate in city decisions concerning the 
annexation and subdivision.

     The Political Reform Act provides that:

     No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has 
a financial interest.

     Government Code Section 87100.

[illegible]

Robert E. Roney
October 17, 1980
Page Two


     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

                              . . .

     (b)  Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000);

                              . . .

                         Government Code Section 87103.

     It cannot be disputed that intensive development of
real property will increase not only its value, but also
the value of property in its vicinity.  This effect is even more
certain with respect to property directly adjacent to the
property being developed.  Neither Mr. Hervey nor Mr. Howard
should, therefore, participate in any city decisions relating
to the subdivision of land adjacent to property they own.
The City's decisions concerning the annexation of the 320
acre parcel are inexorably bound to its decisions concerning
the subdivision.  Clearly, the developer of the 320 acre
parcel wants annexation in order to subdivide, and if the
subdivision was completely rejected, it is doubtful if the
annexation would go forward.  In addition, if neither Mr. Hervey
nor Mr. Howard's property is currently contiguous to the
City, annexation of the 320 acre parcel would make them
contiguous and would therefore make it significantly more
likely that Mr. Hervey's and Mr. Howard's property would
eventually be annexed as well.  Finally, as you have indicated,
the terms and conditions of the annexation will probably be
the same as those for the subdivision, another indication of
the manner in which these two decisions are tied together.

     In closing, note that the Political Reform Act does not
prohibit:

          Appearances by a public official as a member of
     the general public before an agency in the course of
     its prescribed governmental function to represent him-
     self on herself on matters related solely to his or her
     personal interests.

                         2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
                         18700(d)(2) and (f)(1).

Robert E. roney
October 17, 1980
Page Three


Therefore, after they have disqualified themselves as public
officials from participating in the City's decisions concerning
the annexation and subdivision of the 320 acre parcel, Mr. Hervey
and Mr. Howard can appear before City agencies on the same
terms as members of the general public to inform the City of
how they believe these issues will affect their property.
If you have any further questions concerning this matter,
please do not hesitate to contact me again.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Sarah Cameron
Deputy Chief for
Conflicts of Interest
Legal Division

SC:plh

City of Portola
P.O. BOX 1225
PORTOLA, CALIFORNIA 96122
Telephone: (916) 832-4217

September 24, 1980

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca.
95804

Gentlemen;

I am City Attorney for the City of Portola, a general law City of
approximately 2,000 people in Eastern Plumas County.

We have been approached by a local real estate broker about the
possibility of annexation of approximately 320 acres to the City
in connection with a proposed subdivision therein. The proposal
involves a projected mix of residential uses together with a golf
course with related improvements. The projected 10-15 year build-
out would (at 100% buildout) result in +/- 1,200 living units.

The proposed area for annexation and subdivision is currently
uninhabited territory owned by the Forest Service; it has been
declared surplus and the prospective subdivider has lands suitable
for a land exchange to enable him to acquire the proposed sub-
division site.

Two members of the City Council own unimproved land which they
independently acquired at least a year ago which abuts upon the
land proposed as the area of annexation/subdivision. They own
respectively a little more than one acre and a little more than
three acres. The approximate value of the land is $10,000.00 an
acre at current values.

We would like to know if existing conflict law prohibits either
or both councilmembers from voting on either annexation or the
subdivision issues because of their land ownership.

If more facts are needed, please call (916) 832-5845 or (916)
832-4216 during ordinary business hours.

Sincerely,

Robert F. Roney
City Attorney

sections.question: We would like to know if existing conflict law prohibits either or both councilmembers from voting on either annexation or the subdivision issues because of their land ownership.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether two city council members who own unimproved land adjacent to a proposed annexation and subdivision area are prohibited from voting on the annexation and subdivision matters under California conflict of interest law.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No CONCLUSION or ADVICE section present. Document is dated September 24, 1980 and provides detailed factual scenario. Contact information and requestor identification clearly stated.

embedding.summary: A city attorney for Portola, California requests FPPC guidance on whether two city council members who own adjacent land (approximately 1-3 acres valued at ~$10,000/acre) must recuse themselves from voting on a proposed 320-acre annexation and subdivision project involving a real estate broker's residential and golf course development proposal.
